content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children ___ (play) in the garden all morning, and now they are covered in mud.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express duration from a past point up to now with a visible present result
present perfect simple does not emphasise the continuous, ongoing nature of the activity throughout the morning
past continuous describes an activity in progress at a past moment, not one that has continued up to the present

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (study) for his exam since breakfast, so he needs a break now.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot anchor the activity to a past starting point marked by 'since breakfast'
present perfect simple does not convey the uninterrupted, continuous effort over the whole period
past simple marks a completed action and cannot connect to the present need for a break

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My hands are really cold because I ___ (wait) outside for the bus for nearly forty minutes.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express a duration of forty minutes leading up to and explaining the present result of cold hands
present perfect simple does not emphasise the prolonged, continuous nature of the wait that caused the cold hands
past continuous places the action in the past without connecting it to the present visible result

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: She ___ (cough) all day, so she has decided to leave work early and rest.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot describe an activity that has lasted all day up to the present moment of deciding to leave
present perfect simple does not convey the repeated, continuous nature of the coughing throughout the whole day
past perfect continuous places the action before another past event, but the coughing is still relevant now

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: How long ___ you ___ (learn) to drive? Your instructor says you are almost ready for the test.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot form a 'how long' question about a duration stretching from the past to the present
present perfect simple is less natural here because the focus is on the ongoing duration of the learning process, not its completion
past simple cannot be used in a 'how long' question about an activity that is still continuing up to now

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Mark looks exhausted because he ___ (work) on the report without a break since early this morning.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express the unbroken duration from early this morning that explains his current exhausted appearance
present perfect simple does not emphasise the continuous, uninterrupted effort that produced the visible result of exhaustion
the subject is 'he' (third-person singular), so 'have been working' is a subject-verb agreement error

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The neighbours ___ (renovate) their kitchen for three weeks now, and the noise is driving everyone on the street mad.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot link the activity to the three-week duration starting in the past and explain the ongoing present nuisance
present perfect simple suggests the renovation is finished, which contradicts the present tense 'is driving' showing it is still happening
omitting the auxiliary 'have' produces a non-finite form that cannot function as a main verb in this clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna's eyes are red because she ___ (cry), though she says she feels better now.
distractor_rationale: present continuous is contradicted by the clause 'she feels better now', which shows the crying has recently stopped
present perfect simple does not convey the prolonged, repeated nature of the crying that produced the visible result of red eyes
past continuous places the activity in the past without explaining the present visible result of red eyes

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (look) for a bigger flat since our second child was born, but we still haven't found the right one.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot anchor the search to the specific past starting point 'since our second child was born'
present perfect simple does not emphasise the continuous, ongoing nature of the search that is still unresolved now
past simple marks a completed action and is incompatible with 'since' used to mark a duration up to the present

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The floor is wet because the tap ___ (drip) for at least two hours before David noticed it.
distractor_rationale: present perfect continuous cannot be used here because the dripping ended before a specific past event ('before David noticed it'), requiring the past perfect continuous
past continuous does not convey the extended, uninterrupted two-hour duration that led up to the past moment of noticing
past simple does not express the continuous, prolonged nature of the dripping that produced the wet floor before the past discovery

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (study) for his exam since early this morning, and he looks very tired now.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express a duration starting in the past with 'since'; it only describes what is happening right now without anchoring to a past start point
present perfect simple focuses on the completed result, not the ongoing duration; 'since early this morning' emphasises continuity, making the continuous form more natural
the subject 'Tom' is third-person singular and requires 'has', not 'have'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (wait) for the bus for nearly forty minutes, and it still has not arrived.
distractor_rationale: present continuous with 'for nearly forty minutes' cannot express a duration that started in the past and continues now; it lacks the connection to a past starting point
present perfect simple is possible but unnatural here because 'for nearly forty minutes' highlights ongoing duration and continuity, which the continuous form expresses more precisely
past continuous describes an action in progress at a past moment, not one that started in the past and is still continuing now

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Her hands are covered in paint because she ___ (decorate) the living room all afternoon.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot explain a visible present result caused by a past activity; 'all afternoon' signals duration from earlier in the day up to now
the subject 'she' is third-person singular and requires 'has', not 'have'
present perfect simple does not emphasise the ongoing, continuous nature of the activity that produced the visible result; the continuous form is more natural with 'all afternoon'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: How long ___ (you / learn) Spanish? Your pronunciation is already very clear.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot form a 'How long' question about a duration stretching from a past point to the present moment
present perfect simple is grammatically possible but less natural in a 'How long' question about an ongoing skill, where the continuous form emphasises unfinished, repeated practice over time
past continuous forms a 'How long' question about a past moment only and cannot connect to the present result mentioned in the second clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children ___ (run) around the garden since lunch, so they are completely out of breath now.
distractor_rationale: 'know' is a stative verb and cannot be used in any continuous form; using it here produces a grammatically incorrect and semantically nonsensical sentence

present continuous with 'since lunch' cannot express a duration that began at a specific past time and continues to the present, and it does not explain the visible result described in the second clause

present perfect simple does not emphasise the continuous, energetic activity over the whole duration that caused the children to be out of breath; the continuous form is more natural with 'since lunch' and a visible present result

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: My hands are covered in paint because I ___ the kitchen walls all morning.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express an action that has been going on since a past point and explains a present result
present perfect simple does not emphasise the ongoing duration and effort that produced the visible result
omits the -ing inflection on the main verb, producing an ungrammatical form after 'have been'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom looks exhausted because he ___ for his final exams since last Monday.
distractor_rationale: present continuous with 'since' cannot link a past starting point to the current moment
present perfect simple does not convey the continuous effort over the period marked by 'since'
past continuous describes an ongoing action in the past but does not connect to the present result of exhaustion

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The children are very tired because they ___ in the garden for three hours.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express a duration that started in the past and explains a current state
past continuous describes an interrupted past action and does not explain the children's current tiredness
omits 'been', producing an ungrammatical present perfect form without the auxiliary needed for the continuous aspect

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ long have you been waiting for the doctor to call you back?
distractor_rationale: 'What long' is not a grammatical question form in English for asking about duration
'Since' introduces a starting point, not an open question about the length of a period
'For' introduces a specific duration but cannot open an interrogative asking about an unknown length of time

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara's eyes are red and sore because she ___ at a bright screen for over two hours.
distractor_rationale: present continuous with 'for over two hours' cannot anchor the action to a past start point that explains the current result
present perfect simple does not emphasise the uninterrupted duration that caused the soreness
past perfect continuous places the duration entirely before another past event, not before the present moment

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We ___ this apartment since we moved to the city, and we still enjoy living here.
distractor_rationale: present continuous cannot express an unbroken action that began at a specific past point marked by 'since'
present perfect simple is possible here but does not emphasise the continuous, uninterrupted nature of the renting over the whole period
omits the -ing inflection on the main verb, producing an ungrammatical structure after 'have been'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The floor is wet because someone ___ the bathroom tap on for nearly half an hour.
distractor_rationale: present perfect simple describes a completed action and does not convey the continuous duration that caused the floor to become so wet
present continuous cannot express an action that started in the past and has produced a visible present result
past continuous describes an ongoing past action without the connection to the present state of the wet floor

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: James ___ about the same problem at work for weeks, and his colleagues are getting tired of hearing it.
distractor_rationale: simple present describes a habit or general truth but cannot express a continuous action spanning 'weeks' up to now
uses passive voice incorrectly; 'complain' is intransitive and the subject is the agent, not the recipient of the action
present continuous with 'for weeks' cannot anchor the action to a past starting point and emphasise its unbroken duration

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna ___ her new language app every day since she bought it last month, and her vocabulary has already improved.
distractor_rationale: simple present cannot express a continuous activity that began at a specific past point and has produced a present result
'know' is a stative verb and cannot be used in any continuous form; this distractor tests the stative-verb error
simple past places the action entirely in the past and cannot connect it to the present improvement

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The team ___ on this project for six months, so they deserve a break now that it is finished.
distractor_rationale: present perfect simple is possible but does not emphasise the continuous, sustained effort over the full six-month duration
past perfect places the action before another past event, not before the present moment of deserving a break
present continuous with 'for six months' cannot express an action anchored to a past starting point and now recently completed

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She is waiting for the bus for over thirty minutes and looks very tired.
content.error_sentence: She is waiting for the bus for over thirty minutes and looks very tired.
content.corrected_sentence: She has been waiting for the bus for over thirty minutes and looks very tired.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom have been studying for his exam since early this morning.
content.error_sentence: Tom have been studying for his exam since early this morning.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom has been studying for his exam since early this morning.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The children have playing in the garden all afternoon, so their shoes are muddy.
content.error_sentence: The children have playing in the garden all afternoon, so their shoes are muddy.
content.corrected_sentence: The children have been playing in the garden all afternoon, so their shoes are muddy.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I have been knowing Anna since we were at school together, so I trust her completely.
content.error_sentence: I have been knowing Anna since we were at school together, so I trust her completely.
content.corrected_sentence: I have known Anna since we were at school together, so I trust her completely.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: How long has your sister been work at the hospital? She seems exhausted lately.
content.error_sentence: How long has your sister been work at the hospital? She seems exhausted lately.
content.corrected_sentence: How long has your sister been working at the hospital? She seems exhausted lately.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark has worked on that report since nine o'clock this morning and his eyes are red.
content.error_sentence: Mark has worked on that report since nine o'clock this morning and his eyes are red.
content.corrected_sentence: Mark has been working on that report since nine o'clock this morning and his eyes are red.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We are living in this flat for three years now, and we still love the neighbourhood.
content.error_sentence: We are living in this flat for three years now, and we still love the neighbourhood.
content.corrected_sentence: We have been living in this flat for three years now, and we still love the neighbourhood.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa has been wait for her test results since Monday, and she is feeling very anxious.
content.error_sentence: Lisa has been wait for her test results since Monday, and she is feeling very anxious.
content.corrected_sentence: Lisa has been waiting for her test results since Monday, and she is feeling very anxious.
